meta.title: ForgePlayer — Getting Started (Big Buck Bunny)
meta.purpose: Show a new user the get-started flow end to end: add a folder, set up screens + audio outputs once, pick the synthesis algorithm, calibrate, launch, and play in sync. Doubles as a self-contained TEST FIXTURE for the demofoundry pipeline.
meta.voiceStyle: warm, confident, practical — a friend walking you through first-time setup. No hype; just 'here's what you do, and why.'
meta.runtime: self-contained static HTML test app — serve demo/getting-started.html (no backend, no device, no libmpv). Unlike the FunscriptForge tour (which needs the full vite app at :1430), this fixture is one file the runner can serve and drive deterministically.
meta.narrativeSpine: Setup-once, play-forever. Lead with the pain (juggling windows + a stim box), then walk the four tabs in nav order — Library (add a folder) -> Setup (screens + crop, then audio roles) -> Preferences (synthesis algorithm) -> Live (calibrate -> launch -> play) — landing on the payoff: one play button drives every screen and every haptic output, frame for frame.
meta.sceneOrderNote: Scene order follows the app nav: Library -> Setup -> Preferences -> Live. Setup is split into two scenes (screens/crop, then audio roles) because each is its own column and its own idea.
meta.honestyNote: This is a MOCK for demo capture. It shows and animates the UI; it does not play real video, generate a real stim carrier, or drive a device. Narration may say 'press play' / 'the carrier runs alongside' because that's true of the real app — but the fixture itself only animates a transport. Don't imply the HTML is doing real playback.
meta.schemaNotes: Each scene's narrationSec is the AUTHORITY for scene length — the runner holds the scene that long while the voiceover plays, regardless of how fast the mock responds. Actions fire in order and should total <= narrationSec, with a trailing 'hold' filling the remainder. Prefer 'dataTour' selectors (every targeted element in getting-started.html carries data-tour); 'css'/'text' are fallbacks; 'approxXY' (at the declared 1440x900 viewport) is a camera/zoom hint, not a click target. The page also exposes window.__forgeHighlight(name,on) to ring a [data-tour] target without relying on hover.

dataTourHooks.why: Every element the tour targets already carries a stable data-tour attribute in getting-started.html, so this tour is reliable and re-runnable with no extra dev work.

hooks.element: the top tab strip (Library / Setup / Preferences / Live)
the scene-card grid (revealed after Choose folder)
the Big Buck Bunny scene card
Scene audio row (+ its Test button: scene-audio-test)

scenes.title: Welcome
scenes.narration: This is ForgePlayer. If you've ever tried to play a scene across two monitors and an e-stim box by juggling video windows and hoping they stay in sync — this is the fix. One scene folder, one play button, every screen and every haptic output locked together, frame for frame. Let me show you how to get started.

target.region: soft sweep across the four tabs to imply the whole flow

scenes.title: Add a folder → the Library
scenes.narration: Everything starts with a folder. I point ForgePlayer at a folder of my scenes, and it scans them into a library. Each card pulls a real frame from the video, and the little badges tell me which device families that scene already has scripts for — e-stim, Handy, OSR2, SR6. We'll use Big Buck Bunny — it's royalty-free, so it's safe to show. The important idea: everything about a scene — the video, the audio, the funscripts for every channel — travels together as one card. Nothing to wire up by hand.

target.region: Big Buck Bunny card — thumbnail + device badges

actions.note: linger so the thumbnail + the four device badges read on camera

scenes.title: Setup → screens & crop
scenes.narration: First-time setup, done once. Setup is where I tell ForgePlayer about my rig. I pick which monitors are for playback — here, my two external screens — and keep the controls on my laptop, so it stays free. One decoder, several screens, all in sync. My middle screen is an ultrawide. Instead of living with black bars, I turn on Crop — it scales the video to fill the monitor's aspect ratio. And I choose where the crop sits: keep the top of the frame, the center, or the bottom, each with a small margin so nobody gets sliced off at the edge. Center is the default, and it's the right choice most of the time. That's the whole screen story — set it once, and ForgePlayer remembers it for every scene from now on.

actions.note: as VO says 'which monitors are for playback'
pan the three radios as the VO names them

scenes.title: Setup → audio device roles
scenes.narration: Now the outputs — and this is the part that replaces the device-picker dance. Scene audio, the video's own sound, goes to my speakers or headphones. I can Test any output right here to confirm it's alive before I commit. Haptic 1 is my main e-stim output — the USB dongle. Its Test plays a short, gentle stim sample, so I know the signal is reaching the box before a scene ever starts. Haptic 2 is an optional second stim output — a prostate channel, or a second device entirely. And if you plug something in after launch, Refresh devices re-scans without a restart. Assign each role once; ForgePlayer routes every scene to the right output automatically.

scenes.title: Preferences → synthesis algorithm
scenes.narration: One choice matters for how the stim actually feels: the generation algorithm. Continuous is the classic waveform — pick it for a 312 or a 2B. Pulse-based is power-efficient and slower to numb; it's built for modern audio-based stereostim like the Tingler or EstimHero, and it's ForgePlayer's default. Two clicks, set once. And if your dongle or electrode placement runs a hair ahead of or behind the video, the haptic offset nudges the stim into sync. That's every preference that matters for getting started.

actions.note: linger on the default + its 'default' pill

scenes.title: Go Live → calibrate & launch
scenes.narration: Setup is done — now we go Live. ForgePlayer already knows my screens and my outputs, so the Live panel stays clean: just the scene and the controls. Before I launch, I calibrate each haptic output — a quick level check, so the stim arrives at a comfortable strength, not a surprise. One for Haptic 1, one for Haptic 2. Fullscreen players takes each window edge-to-edge on its monitor — kiosk mode, no chrome — and it's live, so I can flip it even while a scene is playing. Everything's set: screens chosen, outputs tested and calibrated, the algorithm picked. There's nothing left to wire up. So I press Launch — and ForgePlayer opens a player on each screen I chose, all pointed at the same scene, all waiting on a single play button. This is the moment the whole rig becomes one instrument.

target.region: Launch — reveals the live player wall

scenes.title: Play — every screen in lockstep
scenes.narration: And there it is — one scene, every screen, in lockstep. The ultrawide is cropped to fill, the side screens are pillar-free, and they're all decoding the same timeline. I press play once, and everything moves together. Seek anywhere on the bar, jump to the next chapter or the previous one — every window and every output stays frame-accurate. No resync, no drift, no chasing windows around. And on the haptic outputs, the stim carrier is running right alongside the picture — the same scene driving what you see and what you'd feel. From here you just watch. The juggling act is gone.

target.region: play button — starts the synced transport
the three screens playing in sync (center cropped to fill)
seek bar filling as it plays

scenes.title: Close
scenes.narration: That's how you get started with ForgePlayer. Point it at a folder, set up your screens and outputs once, and from then on every scene plays everywhere at once — video, sound, and e-stim, perfectly in sync. One player for the whole rig. Add your scenes, press play, and enjoy. Welcome to ForgePlayer.
